questions.question: A brute-force ray tracer checks every primitive for every ray. How does a BVH reduce the number of intersection tests per ray?
options: It sorts primitives by distance so closer ones are tested first
It organizes primitives into a tree of bounding boxes; if a ray misses a parent box, it skips testing all primitives in that subtree
It replaces all primitives with approximations that are faster to intersect
It uses the GPU to test all primitives in parallel instead of sequentially
questions.explanation: The BVH groups primitives into AABBs in a tree. When a ray misses a parent AABB, the entire subtree is pruned — no primitives in that subtree are tested. This reduces tests from O(n) to O(log n) in practice.

questions.question: The SAH (Surface Area Heuristic) estimates the cost of a split as: C = C_trav + P(hit_left) * C_left + P(hit_right) * C_right. What does P(hit_left) represent and how is it computed?
options: The probability a random ray hits any primitive in the left subtree, computed as the number of left primitives divided by total primitives
The probability a random ray hits the left child's AABB, computed as the ratio of the left child's surface area to the parent's surface area
The probability a random ray hits the left child's AABB, computed as the left child's volume divided by the parent's volume
The ratio of the number of left primitives to right primitives, weighted by the distance from the split plane
questions.explanation: SAH models P(hit_left) as the ratio SA(left_child) / SA(parent). The intuition: a randomly oriented ray hitting the parent bounding box is more likely to hit a child with larger surface area. Volume is not used because a ray's chance of hitting a box depends on its exposed surface, not its volume.

questions.question: In the slab method for AABB–ray intersection, you compute t_min and t_max for each of the three axes. Under what condition does the ray miss the AABB?
options: When any individual axis has t_min > t_max for that axis
When the maximum of all enter-t values exceeds the minimum of all exit-t values, or the overall exit is behind the ray
When any axis has a zero-direction component (ray is parallel to that slab)
When the ray origin is outside the AABB on at least one axis
questions.explanation: The ray hits the AABB only if all three slab intervals overlap. This means max(t_x_enter, t_y_enter, t_z_enter) must be less than min(t_x_exit, t_y_exit, t_z_exit), and the exit must be in front of the ray (t_exit > 0). If the overall enter exceeds the overall exit, at least two slabs don't overlap — the ray misses.

questions.question: Why is a BVH generally preferred over a kd-tree for dynamic scenes (where objects move each frame)?
options: BVH trees are always shallower than kd-trees, so traversal is faster
BVH uses object partitioning — each primitive lives in exactly one node — so moving objects requires only refitting AABBs rather than rebuilding spatial cells
kd-trees cannot handle spheres and only work with triangles
BVH construction is O(n) while kd-tree construction is O(n²), making BVH cheaper to rebuild
questions.explanation: In a BVH, each primitive belongs to exactly one leaf node (object partitioning). When objects move, you can refit — just recompute the AABB bounds bottom-up without changing the tree topology. kd-trees use spatial partitioning; a moving object may cross split planes, requiring a full rebuild of the affected subtrees.

questions.question: A kd-tree and a BVH both accelerate ray-scene intersection. Which statement correctly describes the key structural difference?
options: A BVH splits space into non-overlapping cells; a kd-tree groups objects into overlapping bounding volumes

A BVH groups primitives into bounding volumes (object partitioning — siblings may overlap spatially); a kd-tree splits space into non-overlapping cells (spatial partitioning — no overlap but primitives may appear in multiple cells)
A BVH only works with axis-aligned splits; a kd-tree can split along arbitrary planes
A BVH always has exactly log₂(n) depth; a kd-tree depth depends on the scene geometry
questions.explanation: BVH is object-partitioning: each primitive is in exactly one node, but sibling AABBs can spatially overlap. kd-trees are spatial-partitioning: space is divided into non-overlapping cells, but a large primitive straddling a split plane must be stored in multiple cells. This is the fundamental tradeoff: BVH has lower memory and easier updates; kd-tree has cleaner spatial separation.

questions.question: For a scene with only 5 triangles, a student builds a BVH and observes that ray tracing with the BVH is actually slower than brute force. What is the most likely explanation?
options: The BVH was constructed incorrectly — it should always be faster
The BVH traversal overhead (AABB intersection tests per node) exceeds the cost of directly testing all 5 triangles; acceleration structures only help when n is large enough
AABB intersection is an O(n²) algorithm, making it slower for small n
The slab method has a bug that makes it slower for fewer than 10 primitives
questions.explanation: Acceleration structures add overhead per node visited (AABB test, branching, stack operations). For very small scenes, testing all 5 primitives directly (5 ray-triangle tests) is cheaper than traversing 3–4 BVH nodes (3–4 AABB tests) plus the leaf tests. BVHs typically become beneficial at 100+ primitives.
